agent_reply:
Hi Albert,
 
Thanks for your detailed message.
 
To answer your question directly:
Hiver Automations cannot use your external API’s response within the same automation workflow to assign or tag conversations.
 
However, your backend can absolutely call Hiver’s REST API afterward to update the conversation’s assignee and tags. The endpoints you mentioned (such as assignee.email and tags.to_apply / tags.to_remove) are supported for this.
 
Should you need any further help please reach out to us. 
 
Have a good day ahead!
 
Thanks,

customer_msg: Thank you both for your help
agent_reply:
Hi Mike,

Thank you for your patience while we reviewed this in detail.

I want to clearly explain what we observed and why this is happening.

Before Hiver was installed, Gmail was visually grouping certain
CRM-generated emails together in your personal inbox because they shared
the same subject line. However, these emails were not actual replies to one
another — they did not contain the technical reply references (such as
In-Reply-To or References headers) that indicate a true email conversation.

When Hiver is active, it validates whether emails grouped by Gmail are
genuinely part of the same reply chain. If emails are grouped only by
subject and not by proper reply linkage, Hiver separates them into
individual conversations. This is intentional and expected behavior.

We confirmed this by testing the same CRM emails:

 -

 With Hiver access enabled, the emails were split into individual
 conversations.
 -

 Once Hiver access was revoked, Gmail reverted to grouping them together
 again based purely on subject.

The reason we apply this correction is to ensure long-term conversation
accuracy and data integrity. Allowing unrelated emails to remain merged can
lead to broken threads, incorrect replies, or sync issues later —
especially if such conversations are ever added to a shared mailbox. To
prevent those scenarios, this validation runs at the account level when
Hiver is active.

Importantly, this does not mean Hiver is modifying the emails themselves or
controlling personal inbox behavior. It is correcting Gmail’s visual
grouping when the underlying emails are not actually part of the same
conversation.

I understand how this change in behavior can feel disruptive, especially
since Gmail previously presented these emails as a single thread. While the
behavior you’re seeing is by design, we do recognize the impact it has had
on your workflow, and we appreciate you raising it with us.

If your CRM can be configured to include proper reply references in these
automated emails, Gmail — and Hiver — will naturally keep them in a single
thread. We’re also happy to review a sample with you or your CRM team if
that helps.

Please let me know if you’d like to discuss this further or if there’s
anything specific you’d like us to look at next. We’re here to help and
want to make sure you’re comfortable moving forward.

Regards,

*Harsha G*

Support Advisor | Hiver Support

• Everything worked correctly until we removed team@ as a paid user and
added claudia@ in its place
• After that change, emails sent to af@ began appearing in both inboxes
• We added Claudia solely for access to the external knowledge base. We

were not able to make this happen yesterday.

Kate Cardwell

866-749-0025 ext 701
443-473-6315 mobile
agent_reply:
Hi Kate,
 
Thank you for the detailed summary, that’s very helpful.
 
Regarding your questions:
Claudia would not be able to log in to Hiver using the team@ account. Since team@ is configured as a shared inbox address, it isn’t meant to be used as a Hiver user login, and the external knowledge base wouldn’t be accessible from that account. Claudia should instead access the knowledge base using her own Hiver user account.
The change made yesterday was limited to adjusting the user seat so Claudia could be added. This change itself would not require adding additional users or moving to another user tier.
 
That said, the behaviour you’re seeing with emails sent to af@ appearing in both the AF and Team inboxes is something we’d like to review more closely. I’ve discussed this with the colleague who assisted earlier, and we’d like to walk through the current Gmail alias and shared inbox setup with you to better understand what may be causing this.
 
In the meantime, could you also share the Message-ID of one of the emails sent to af@ that appeared in both inboxes? This will help us trace how the email was delivered.
 
You can find it by following these steps:
Open the email and click the three dots in the top-right corner of the message.
Click on “Show original.”
A new window will open with the full message headers.
Please copy the Message-ID (shown in green at the top of the page) and share it with us.
 
Once you’ve had a chance to send that across, we can review it on our end.
